464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented flow from signup → first class → retention checkpoint (kills early churn)
- **Cancellation & churn**: no standardised win-back or exit interview process; revenue leakage untracked
- **Member check-ins**: no cadence or framework for proactive engagement between classes
- **Coaching standards & quality audit**: no observable criteria for what "good coaching" looks like at The Forge; no audit mechanism
- **New quarter program launch**: no repeatable calendar or comms sequence for rolling out new classes/programming
- **GDPR & member data privacy**: legal/compliance risk; especially critical for women-only studio with sensitive health data
- **Complaints & incidents**: no formal escalation, documentation, or resolution SOP (safeguarding + reputation risk)
- **Supply chain**: "Order Supplies & Sundries" missing—no reorder triggers, vendor management, or inventory thresholds
- **Finance**: no documented AR follow-up, refund/credit policy, or month-end close process visible
- **Staff scheduling & absence cover**: no documented fallback when coach calls in sick or member-facing role gaps occur

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding (Coaches)**: likely missing induction to The Forge's brand voice, member communication standards, safeguarding expectations
- **Class scheduling**: no mention of how subs/cover instructors are assigned or communicated to members
- **Marketing SOPs (5/5 complete)**: check whether they reference member referral incentives or win-back campaigns—if not, gap between marketing plan and execution
- **Facilities (5/6)**: missing deep-clean/hygiene audit cadence and post-incident sanitisation protocol
- **Pause/suspension member flow**: no clarity on how paused members are re-engaged or

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REVIEW (June 2026)

## REVENUE — INCREASE FOCUS (This is the priority)

• **Pricing & AR/M drift is the #1 concern.** Revenue per member (AR/M) has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop. You've added 55 net members since December but revenue hasn't grown proportionally. Either your new intake are on discounted rates, or class pack upsells/PT aren't tracking. Audit June cohort pricing vs December; raise new member entry price by 10–15% immediately and test £5–10 upsells (PT taster, nutrition plan, merchandise).

• **May→June: Revenue fell 12% (£20.2k → £17.7k) despite adding 25 members.** This is pure pricing/mix erosion, not member count. Non-people expenses held flat (sensible), but you're not monetising the bigger studio's capacity. Implement class-based upsells (peak time premium tier, off-peak discounts for retention, not acquisition).

• **Churn spiked to 5.74% in May then flatlined at 0% in June — suspect data or retention cliff.** Either June churn is delayed reporting or you fixed something. Clarify: if real, replicate it; if data issue, fix tracking. A typical fitness gym targets 3–4% churn; 0% suggests underreporting. Even at 3%, you're losing £5.4k+ annual revenue (36 × £150 avg member value). Launch a retention win-back campaign: 3-month loyalty bonus or friend referral (cost <£2 per member, lifetime value gain ~£200).

---

## EXPENSES — REDUCE DRAG

• **Non-people costs spiralled Jan–April (£8.2k → £30.5k) then settled at £9.4k in June — the April fit-out distortion is now clear and normalising.** Good. June non-people (£9,390) is 53% of revenue: *benchmark is 25–35% for a fitness gym.* This is high. Audit: utilities (heating/lighting the new space?), rent, insurance, supplies. If rent doubled with the studio, that's the fix — consider sub-letting unused capacity or tiered membership to cover it. If utilities, install LED/smart thermostats.

• **People costs are creeping: £3,686 (June) vs £3,550 (May) — 3.8% month-on-month.**

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her 17 Build: Lower and 9 Build: Upper sessions must shift to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo is already running 15 Upper and can absorb the Upper slots; Eilis can take Lower (her weakest class type currently) as a development opportunity, or split both classes with JoJo.

• **Rebalance JoJo's PT load**: JoJo is carrying 44 PTs while Eilis has only 11. Move 10–15 of JoJo's PT clients to Eilis to free JoJo's schedule and develop Eilis as a stronger all-round coach; JoJo remains the specialist for complex cases.

• **Cover Grace's No Sweat Intro and Strongevity**: The 3 No Sweat Intro sessions can go to either coach (low technical bar); the 3 Strongevity sessions are a gap — assign to JoJo or introduce Eilis to this format if it fits her development plan.

• **Stop treating Body Composition scans as coaching load**: Don't use them to balance the timetable. Spread InBody duties evenly across all coaches as admin, or assign to one person monthly to keep the coaching picture clear.

• **Target JoJo at 110–115 sessions/month**: Her 133 is unsustainable. Once Grace's hours are reassigned and PT load shared, she should stabilise around Head Coach duties, Tuesdays, specialist PT, and Build: Upper — the roles only she can do.

• **Check Eilis's progression path**: She now has 48 sessions plus incoming hours from Grace/JoJo. Agree with her on which class types and PT she'll develop into, and monitor whether 70–80 sessions/month is her ceiling

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps (between PT and classes):**

1. **Launch a Monday lunchtime open-gym slot** — Monday is your quietest day overall; a 45-minute unstructured lift window (11:30–12:15) with a staff member present captures the corporate crowd who can't commit to full classes.

2. **Add a Saturday morning "Strongevity + Coffee" social workshop** — Strongevity has only 32 attendees over 90 days; pair a 30-minute technique clinic on longevity training with 15 minutes of informal networking and coffee on Saturday mornings to rebuild community around this class and fill dead time.

**To lift the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Engine to a Thursday or Friday evening slot and run a 2-week "Engine Challenge" with incentive prizes** — Engine (55 attendees, 14 last month) is severely underperforming; it's likely a timing problem. Reposition it during your busier mid-week window and promote it as a 30-day conditioning challenge with small rewards (free PT session, merch) to create momentum and social proof.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**1. Map what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
Before you design menus, spend 2 hours documenting every service your coaches currently deliver — group classes, 1:1 training, nutrition consults, form checks, whatever. Include how long each takes. This prevents the menu from being aspirational fiction. Ask your top 3 coaches what they do that members ask for most.

**2. Create two separate, visually distinct documents**
— **Coach menu**: Lists service codes, time blocks, pricing structure, what you charge members vs. what coaches retain. This removes negotiation friction when selling. **Member menu**: Shows what they *experience* (e.g., "6-week strength block," not "12 one-to-ones"). Include what results they should expect. Make it Instagram-storyable; it's a sales tool.

**3. Price by anchoring to your best service, not guessing**
Pick your highest-demand offering (probably small-group training or 1:1). Research 3 competitors in Belfast + London. Price yours 10-15% *higher* if retention/community is stronger. Set everything else relative to that anchor. Print test pricing on mock menus — you'll spot gaps immediately.

**4. Build in a "try before you buy" hook**
Add a single low-friction entry point (free form audit, one subsidised class, one discounted intro session). Hardlink it to the member menu. This converts browsers into paying members faster than any pricing gimmick.

**5. Version it for actual use TODAY**
Laminate a coach copy for the gym floor. Upload member version to your site by Friday. Any delay and you've lost

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

You've cracked lead volume and cost efficiency—July's £2/lead is a 75% improvement on June and 97% cheaper than April—but you've hit a cliff on conversion. Your close rate has collapsed from 29% (Apr) to 4% (Jul). You're now flooding the funnel with low-intent leads; the math is brutal: 165 leads at 4% close nets just £1,323 revenue against £376 spend, whereas April's 69 leads at 29% close generated the same income on similar spend and left room for profit scaling.

The trend suggests your targeting or creative has drifted away from high-intent women (likely trading intent for volume via broader audiences, lookalikes, or interest-stacking that no longer filters for gym-ready prospects). Your July CPL is artificially low because you're reaching people unlikely to convert—the algorithm is rewarding you for clicks, not business results.

**Actions**

1. **Revert to April's audience architecture immediately.** Pull the exact targeting, lookalike seed, and exclusions from your April campaign. Run a small test spend (£80–100) to confirm 4–6% CPL with 20%+ close rate returns. April worked; document it and rebuild there before testing incrementally outward.

2. **Kill or pause the July creative.** A5-fold jump in leads on the same budget means your ad creative or hook shifted to something too broad ("free trial," generic fitness language). Audit your July copy, images, and CTA against April's. Rebuild two variants that emphasise exclusivity, women-only community, and transformation proof (testimonials, before/afters of real members). Test these at 50% of budget.

3. **Tighten the top-of-funnel gate.** Your lead form is catching tyre-kickers. Add a qualifying question—"Have you trained before?" or "Are you ready to start in the next 2 weeks?"—to the form or use a lead quality filter in your pixel. Target only age 25–55, exclude past non-converters and cold audiences older than 180 days. Aim to halve lead volume but double conversion.

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO PRIORITY ACTIONS

## REVENUE — CRITICAL FOCUS

• **Churn spike in May (12.7%) is eroding growth.** You gained 14 members Apr–May but lost 17 to churn; Jun recovered to 3.3% but base sits at 138. Root cause: post-relocation friction, pricing tolerance, or class scheduling. Action: audit May joiners' class attendance in first 30 days, survey churners on reason (experience/price/schedule), and implement 90-day "new member success" check-ins. Target: hold churn at ≤4% baseline.

• **Rev/member has fallen to £114 (June) from £138 (April).** This is a £24/member/month erosion — likely mix (more entry-level members post-relocation?) and low card/upsell capture. Action: audit current member pricing bands; implement class-card upsells (10x/month, 20x/month bundles) at £25–£45 premium, target +£8–£12 Rev/member. On 138 members, that's +£1,100–£1,650/month.

• **April–June revenue flat at £15.7k–£16.9k despite 138 active members.** Compare Feb (£15.5k on 119 members = strong conversion). Action: push new studio opening in June comms — run "bring a friend free" offer for July targeting 150+ members, and backfill quiet off-peak slots (e.g. 10:00am Mon–Fri) with discount drop-in pricing (£8 vs £12) to fill capacity and build habit.

## EXPENSES — IMMEDIATE REVIEW

• **June cash out is £20.6k (Feb was £16.5k, March was £23.3k post-relocation).** Excluding April's exceptional spend, you're running £16.6k–£20.6k/month outflow. On £15.7k revenue, you're negative or razor-thin. Action: produce a detailed P&L breakdown (rent, payroll, utilities, supplies, insurance). If post-relocation rent has lifted, model break-even at 160+ members to justify the step-up. If payroll is bloated, confirm staffing headcount vs revenue per FTE — target ratio of 3–4 members per staff hour worked.

• **May net cash was positive (£3.6k) but June swung to -£2.5k** despite similar revenue. Investigate: delayed payables timing, utility bill, or stock
